milestones.vision: ProfileConfigLoader discovers *.env files (application.env, application-{profile}.env) alongside existing config formats, parses them with a dedicated DotEnvParser, wraps them as EnvPropertySource so relaxed binding (MY_APP_PORT → my.app.port) works identically to process.env, and slots them between process.env and regular config files in the precedence chain — the standard dotenv convention.
success_criteria: application.env and application-{profile}.env are discovered in config/ and cwd by ProfileConfigLoader
Parsed values are wrapped in EnvPropertySource, giving full relaxed binding (UPPER_SNAKE → dotted.lower)
Precedence: overrides > process.env > *.env files > profile configs > default configs > fallback
DotEnvParser handles: KEY=VALUE, export KEY=VALUE, quoted values (single + double), inline comments on unquoted values, escape sequences in double-quoted values, blank lines, # comment lines
All existing ProfileConfigLoader tests continue to pass
New tests cover .env discovery, parsing, relaxed binding, precedence, and profile-specific .env files

key_risks.risk: DotEnvParser scope creep — .env format has many edge cases (multi-line values, variable interpolation)

key_risks.risk: Format ordering in FORMATS array affects precedence between .env and other formats at the same config-file level
key_risks.whyItMatters: Must insert .env after process.env but before all file-based config formats in the source chain, not just the FORMATS array

proof_strategy.whatWillBeProven: Parser handles all standard .env format cases: bare, quoted, export-prefixed, inline comments, escapes
process.env beats .env file; .env file beats application.json; profile .env beats base .env

milestones.vision: Introduce a single, framework-agnostic middleware pipeline that threads cross-cutting concerns — authentication, observability, error handling, and custom 404 — across all 7 server and serverless adapters through the same CDI component convention already used for controllers. Zero changes to controller code; zero new framework dependencies.
success_criteria: A MiddlewarePipeline compose utility lives in packages/boot and is unit tested in isolation
CDI components opt in via static __middleware = { order: N } — symmetric with __routes
Three built-in middleware components (RequestLoggerMiddleware, ErrorHandlerMiddleware, NotFoundMiddleware) are wired by each *Starter() and disableable via config
All 7 adapters (Express, Fastify, Koa, Hono, Lambda, CF Workers, Azure Fn) thread the pipeline without duplicating error/404 logic
Existing controller tests continue to pass unmodified
An example demonstrates auth + logging + error handling end-to-end

key_risks.risk: Server adapters (Express/Fastify/Hono) need middleware to fire before route matching for auth short-circuit
key_risks.whyItMatters: If auth runs after routing, a matched but unauthorised route still touches the handler — timing matters for auditability and performance

key_risks.risk: Middleware ordering across CDI boot is non-trivial — components scan order is not guaranteed to match desired pipeline order
key_risks.whyItMatters: Wrong order means error handler fires before auth, or logger misses early exits

key_risks.risk: Koa has its own onion model — wrapping with a second compose layer may conflict or double-catch errors
key_risks.whyItMatters: Silent swallowing of errors or double-logging would regress existing behaviour

proof_strategy.riskOrUnknown: Middleware fires before route matching in server adapters
proof_strategy.whatWillBeProven: Test that an auth middleware returning 401 prevents the controller handler from being called at all
Unit test that middlewares are composed in ascending __middleware.order, regardless of CDI scan order
Koa integration tests pass with pipeline threading; error thrown inside handler surfaces as 500 exactly once

milestones.verification_operational: RequestLoggerMiddleware emits method/path/status/duration on every request; ErrorHandlerMiddleware converts thrown errors to structured JSON; NotFoundMiddleware returns consistent 404 JSON
milestones.verification_uat: Example app boots with auth + logging + custom error handler; curl requests confirm: valid auth → 200, missing auth → 401, unknown route → 404, handler throw → 500 with JSON body

# S01: DotEnvParser + ProfileConfigLoader .env integration

**.env file support added to @alt-javascript/config with full relaxed binding and correct 7-layer precedence**

## What Happened

Implemented full .env file support in @alt-javascript/config across four tasks. DotEnvParser handles the complete standard .env format. ProfileConfigLoader discovers application.env and application-{profile}.env via the same SEARCH_DIRS loop, parses them with DotEnvParser, and wraps them in EnvPropertySource so UPPER_SNAKE_CASE keys get the same relaxed binding as process.env. The .env sources sit between process.env and regular config files in the precedence chain — real environment variables always win. 164 tests pass, 8 new integration tests cover every layer of the 7-level chain.

## Verification

164 tests passing. Full 7-layer precedence chain verified: overrides > process.env > profile.env > base.env > profile.json > base.json > fallback. DotEnvParser 28-case suite covers all format variants.

## Requirements Advanced

None.

## Requirements Validated

None.

## New Requirements Surfaced

None.

## Requirements Invalidated or Re-scoped

None.

## Deviations

None from plan. Minor test authoring fix in T04: removed a key from application.json fixture that was shadowing the fallback assertion.

## Known Limitations

DotEnvParser v1 does not support multiline values (backslash-continuation or newlines inside double quotes) or variable interpolation ($VAR / ${VAR}).

## Follow-ups

None.

## Files Created/Modified

- `packages/config/DotEnvParser.js` — New .env file parser: bare, export-prefixed, double/single-quoted, inline comments, escape sequences
- `packages/config/test/DotEnvParser.spec.js` — 28 unit tests covering all DotEnvParser format rules
- `packages/config/ProfileConfigLoader.js` — Added DotEnvParser import and _loadEnvFiles(); restructured load() to insert .env sources between process.env and profile config files
- `packages/config/index.js` — Added DotEnvParser export
- `packages/config/test/ProfileConfigLoader.spec.js` — Extended with 8 .env integration tests including full 7-layer precedence chain

# S01: DotEnvParser + ProfileConfigLoader .env integration — UAT

**Milestone:** M012

## UAT — .env File Support

### Setup

Create `config/application.env` alongside your existing `config/application.json`:

**Implemented MiddlewarePipeline.compose() and collect() in packages/boot**

## What Happened

Created packages/boot/MiddlewarePipeline.js with two static methods: compose() which builds a recursive dispatch chain from an ordered array of middleware instances, and collect() which scans CDI ApplicationContext components for those bearing static __middleware, sorts by ascending order (Infinity default for unordered), and returns the sorted instances. The compose/collect separation is intentional — compose executes in array order (pure), collect handles the CDI scan and sort.

tasks.description:
Create packages/boot/MiddlewarePipeline.js.

Design:
- `MiddlewarePipeline.compose(middlewareInstances, finalHandler)` returns `(request) => Promise<response>`
- Sorts instances by ascending `instance.constructor.__middleware.order` (default order: Infinity so unordered middleware goes last)
- Composes the chain: each middleware receives `(request, next)` where `next` is the remainder of the chain
- `next` can be called with a modified request; returning without calling `next` short-circuits
- `finalHandler` is the innermost fn: `(request) => Promise<response>`
- `MiddlewarePipeline.collect(applicationContext)` scans CDI components for those whose class has `static __middleware`, sorts by order, returns instances

Edge cases:
- Empty middleware list → finalHandler called directly
- Middleware that throws → exception propagates (ErrorHandlerMiddleware catches it at a higher level)
- Middleware that returns undefined after calling next → treated as pass-through (returns the inner result)

tasks.description:
Create packages/boot/middleware/RequestLoggerMiddleware.js

- static __middleware = { order: 10 }
- Reads CDI logger via setApplicationContext / this._applicationContext
- On handle(request, next): records start time, calls next, logs: `[METHOD] path → statusCode (Xms)` at 'verbose' level
- If next throws, logs the error at 'error' level then re-throws (ErrorHandlerMiddleware catches it)
- Config flag: `middleware.requestLogger.enabled` (default true); skip install if false

Create packages/boot/middleware/ErrorHandlerMiddleware.js

- static __middleware = { order: 20 }
- On handle(request, next): wraps next in try/catch; on throw returns { statusCode: err.statusCode || 500, body: { error: err.message } }
- Logs error via CDI logger at 'error' level before returning
- Config flag: `middleware.errorHandler.enabled` (default true)

Create packages/boot/middleware/NotFoundMiddleware.js

- static __middleware = { order: 30 }
- On handle(request, next): calls next; if result is null/undefined or statusCode === 404, returns { statusCode: 404, body: { error: 'Not found' } }
- Actually: acts as innermost pre-handler — if no route matched the adapter sets a sentinel; NotFoundMiddleware produces the 404 response
- Simpler model: NotFoundMiddleware is the last in the chain (highest order), calls next; the adapter's route dispatch is the finalHandler. If no route matches, dispatch returns null and NotFoundMiddleware converts to 404.

Create packages/boot/middleware/index.js that exports all three.

tasks.description:
Refactor LambdaAdapter.handle() to thread the pipeline.

1. In constructor (or init), call `MiddlewarePipeline.collect(applicationContext)` to get sorted middleware instances
2. Build a `_dispatch(request)` method that does the current route-matching + handler call (returning null when no route matches)
3. `handle(event, lambdaContext)` builds the normalised request object, then calls `MiddlewarePipeline.compose(this._middlewares, this._dispatch.bind(this))(request)`
4. Remove the duplicated try/catch and inline 404 from handle() — those are now ErrorHandlerMiddleware and NotFoundMiddleware
5. _normalizeResponse() remains; the pipeline result goes through it

Import MiddlewarePipeline from @alt-javascript/boot.

tasks.description:
Refactor ControllerRegistrar.register() in boot-express.

Approach: the pipeline wraps each route handler call in ControllerRegistrar.
- Accept a `middlewares` array parameter (from MiddlewarePipeline.collect)
- Wrap the bound handler: `const pipelined = MiddlewarePipeline.compose(middlewares, boundHandler)`
- For Express, the request object already has params/query/body/headers; build a normalised request from req before calling pipelined
- The pipeline result needs to be written to res: if { statusCode, body } write that, else res.json(result)
- Remove the try/catch in the route wrapper — ErrorHandlerMiddleware handles it
- Remove the inline error handler middleware added by ExpressAdapter — NotFoundMiddleware/ErrorHandlerMiddleware replace it

Update ExpressAdapter.init() to collect middleware and pass to ControllerRegistrar.

Express-specific: for global middleware (like auth) to fire before routing, add a pre-route hook via app.use() that runs non-route-specific middleware.

decisions.choice: Spring Framework core + Spring Boot core only
decisions.rationale: User explicitly excluded non-core modules (WebMVC, Data, Security). Focus on IoC, DI, config, logging, lifecycle, events, AOP.
Ideological commitment — isomorphic flat ESM in browser without build step. Differentiator vs InversifyJS/NestJS/tsyringe.
Primary consumers are author's own projects. Quirks like null-matching autowire and duplicated global-ref code can be redesigned.
Not a formal part of Spring, standalone port, out of scope for core framework work.
Stage 3 decorators not in JS engines yet (Node 24 Symbol.metadata is undefined). Pure JS constraint means no transpiler fallback.
decisions.revisable: Yes — when decorators reach Stage 4 and ship in engines

decisions.rationale: Prototype proves viability: 8 cross-package tests, eliminates 4 copies of global-ref code, enables coordinated versioning, breaks circular test deps. Each package stays independently publishable.

decisions.choice: JS Proxy — no subclassing or bytecode generation
decisions.rationale: Proxy is native, performant for non-hot-path use, and provides clean before/after/around semantics. PoC proves all 5 advice types work.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if performance benchmarks show issues in hot paths

decisions.choice: Custom isomorphic event bus (no Node EventEmitter)
decisions.rationale: Must work in browser ESM. Simple Map-based pub/sub is sufficient and portable. PoC proves typed events, wildcards, lifecycle events.

Most annotation-like pure JS approach. Co-located with class, no tooling. Consumer provides class array (no filesystem scanning — ESM has no classpath). PoC proves integration with ApplicationContext.
decisions.revisable: Yes — when native decorators ship, could offer decorator alternative

decisions.choice: New repo with fresh git history
decisions.rationale: Clean break for v3.0. No git history baggage from 4 separate repos. Old repos archived. Package names stay the same — only repo structure changes.
Idiomatic JS, works with all underlying drivers. Sync JDBC model doesn't translate to JS ecosystem.
Familiar to Java devs, extensible, driver self-registration via subprotocol.
Spring-familiar, unambiguous, easy to parse.
Own the name — JS port identity, not a fake JDBC.

decisions.choice: JSDBC repo = core interfaces + drivers only
decisions.rationale: Template layer belongs in boot/altjs monorepo where it integrates with CDI, config, lifecycle.
13.5k stars, broad compat, isomorphic SQL in browser.

decisions.choice: tarn.js (proven, Knex uses it in production)
decisions.rationale: Battle-tested, minimal API surface, well-maintained.
pg/mysql/mssql/oracle tests need Docker. CI must pass without them.
Thin bridge to Express/Fastify/etc. Same service layer, framework-specific controllers.
Declarative and imperative both supported.
Use each framework's idiom, don't fight it.

decisions.choice: CDI boots once on first invocation, reuses via closure
decisions.rationale: Standard Lambda warm-reuse pattern.

decisions.choice: 7 adapters: Express, Fastify, Koa, Hono, Lambda, CF Workers, Azure Functions
decisions.rationale: Covers all mainstream JS HTTP targets. Hapi excluded (declining). NestJS/Next.js excluded (different abstraction level).
decisions.revisable: Yes — add adapters as platforms emerge

decisions.rationale: Declarative `{ profiles: { urls: { 'localhost:8080': 'dev' } } }` — symmetric with server-side NODE_ACTIVE_PROFILES.

decisions.choice: Framework-native bridges, no runtime dependency on target framework
decisions.rationale: Vue provide/inject, Alpine store, React Context/hooks, Angular providers. All testable without the framework.

decisions.choice: Bump all package versions together on every release, regardless of which packages changed
decisions.rationale: Simpler mental model — the monorepo has one version at any point in time. Eliminates the cognitive overhead of tracking which packages changed and updating cross-package dependency pins selectively. The cost of an unnecessary version bump is negligible; the cost of a missed or mismatched bump is debugging time.

decisions.decision: Monorepo version bump strategy — lockstep versioning
decisions.choice: Bump all package versions together on every release, regardless of which packages changed
decisions.rationale: Mirrors how large multi-module projects like Spring Framework and Apache Camel manage versions: the entire project moves as one version number. Developers can reason about compatibility instantly — if all packages show 3.0.6, they are compatible. No need to track which packages changed in which release or update cross-package dependency pins selectively. The cost (a few unnecessary npm publishes) is negligible; the benefit (zero version matrix confusion) is permanent.

decisions.decision: Middleware architecture for cross-cutting concerns across all server and serverless adapters
decisions.choice: CDI component middleware pipeline using static __middleware = { order: N } convention, composed by MiddlewarePipeline.compose() in packages/boot
decisions.rationale: The onion/compose model (Koa-style) expressed as pure CDI components is the natural fit: symmetric with __routes, no new APIs, works identically across all 7 adapters via a shared normalised request shape. Middleware opts in via static __middleware = { order: N } — lower order = outermost. MiddlewarePipeline.collect() scans CDI context; compose() is pure functional. Three built-in middleware components (RequestLoggerMiddleware order:10, ErrorHandlerMiddleware order:20, NotFoundMiddleware order:30) are wired by all *Starter() functions and individually disableable via config flags.
decisions.revisable: Yes — order numbering convention may be refined; additional built-in middleware may be added
